summary: British contemporary artist, leader of the Young British Artists (YBA). Known for using formaldehyde to preserve animals, the diamond-encrusted skull For the Love of God, and dot/Veil paintings. Associated with patron Charles Saatchi and the Freeze exhibition.

works.name: The Physical Impossibility of Death in the Mind of Someone Living (1991)
works.description: A tiger shark suspended in formaldehyde in a tank. Funded by Charles Saatchi. Part of the Natural History series. Sold to Steven Cohen in 2004; shark was later replaced due to deterioration. Parodied by the Stuckists. Wikipedia

comprehensive_summary:
Damien Hirst is a British contemporary artist and the most prominent member of the Young British Artists (YBA), frequently paired with Tracey Emin. He is most commonly identified by his use of formaldehyde to preserve animals in tanks as part of his Natural History series, most famously a tiger shark in The Physical Impossibility of Death in the Mind of Someone Living (1991), which was funded by advertising magnate Charles Saatchi and included in Saatchi's Sensation exhibition. The shark piece was sold to Steven Cohen in 2004, after which the shark had to be replaced due to deterioration. It was displayed at the Met from 2007 to 2010 alongside Francis Bacon's Head I. The Stuckists parodied it with A Dead Shark Isn't Art at the Stuckism International Gallery. The 'Re-Object' exhibit paired it with giant Hirst paintings like Iodomethane-13c, and Don Thompson's book on art economics is titled in reference to it. Hirst made variations titled The Kingdom and Beautiful Inside My Head Forever. In one exhibit it was installed in front of Marcus Harvey's Myra, a giant portrait made of children's handprints.

Hirst's other major formaldehyde works include Mother and Child Divided (which he said showed 'Life's like this, then it stops'), Away from the Flock (a lamb), The Dream (a unicorn), The Golden Calf (a bull with gold-encrusted horns sold at the controversial Sotheby's 'Beautiful Inside My Head Forever' auction), Adam and Eve (in blue and pink box frames), God Alone Knows (Carrara marble plinths with carcasses in crucifixion poses), the Pursuit of Oblivion (umbrella in formaldehyde at the Gagosian Gallery), Two Fucking and Two Watching (a rotting cow and bull banned by New York City public health officials), and In Nomine Patris (which added formaldehyde to John LeKay's This Is My Body, This Is My Blood). A disk representing the goddess Hathor between two golden horns also appears in formaldehyde.

For the Love of God (2007) is a platinum-cast human skull encrusted with 8,601 diamonds, featuring the pear-shaped 'Skull Star Diamond' at its center. It was displayed in the Tate Modern's Turbine Hall when no Unilever commission was installed, and was also included in a record-breaking Tate Modern retrospective.

Hirst's dot-based paintings include the Pharmaceutical/Spot paintings (over 1,000 works, of which only one has a black dot) and the Veil paintings, which were inspired by Pierre Bonnard and drew accusations of appropriating Aboriginal Australian dot painting in 2018. He also made a Mickey Mouse painting using twelve dots in four colors, sold for charity in 2012.

Other notable works include the butterfly installation In and Out of Love (where 9,000 butterflies were killed at the Tate Modern in 2012), the cigarette-themed sculptures Hell and The Acquired Inability to Escape, and Verity (a large sculpture of a pregnant woman with a visible fetus in Devon, inspired by Degas's Little Dancer of Fourteen Years). Banksy referenced Hirst in Keep it Spotless, depicting a maid sweeping under a Hirst painting.

Hirst curated the influential Freeze exhibition and Jay Jopling's White Cube gallery hosted shows by YBA artists. He staged the Venice exhibition 'Treasures from the Wreck of the Unbelievable' (disguised as an ancient Greek shipwreck display), which was criticized by Victor Ehikhamenor for copying a Yoruba sculpture for Golden Heads (Female). He created the butterfly-and-pills-themed Empathy Suite hotel room in Las Vegas, was accused of plagiarism by John LeKay over the Spiritus Callidus series, reportedly admires Piero Manzoni's Artist's Breath, and Truong Tran protested his use of live butterflies with pornographic clippings. Jonathan Yeo painted a portrait of Hirst wearing a hazmat suit.
